subject: Physics
q: A body projected vertically upwards with a certain speed from the top of a tower reaches the ground in $t_1$. If it is projected vertically downwards from the same point with the same speed, it reaches the ground in $t_2$. Time required to reach the ground, if it is dropped from the top of the tower, is :
question: A body projected vertically upwards with a certain speed from the top of a tower reaches the ground in $t_1$. If it is projected vertically downwards from the same point with the same speed, it reaches the ground in $t_2$. Time required to reach the ground, if it is dropped from the top of the tower, is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{t}_1=\frac{\mathrm{u}+\sqrt{\mathrm{u}^2+2 \mathrm{gh}}}{\mathrm{g}} \\ & \mathrm{t}_2=\frac{-\mathrm{u}+\sqrt{\mathrm{u}^2+2 \mathrm{gh}}}{\mathrm{g}} \\ & \mathrm{t}=\frac{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{gh}}}{\mathrm{g}} \\ & \mathrm{t}_1 \mathrm{t}_2=\frac{\left(\mathrm{u}^2+2 \mathrm{gh}\right)-\mathrm{u}^2}{\mathrm{~g}^2}=\frac{2 \mathrm{gh}}{\mathrm{g}^2}=\mathrm{t}^2 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}=\sqrt{\mathrm{t}_1 \mathrm{t}_2}\end{aligned}$

question: The relation between time $t$ and distance $\mathrm{x}$ is $\mathrm{t}=a \mathrm{x}^2+\mathrm{bx}$ where $\mathrm{a}$ and $\mathrm{b}$ are constants. The acceleration is

$$
t=a x^2+b x
$$
by differentiating acceleration $=-2 a v^3$

q: Motion of a particle in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>y</mi></math> plane is described by a set of following equations <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo>-</mo><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The path of the particle will be
question: Motion of a particle in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>y</mi></math> plane is described by a set of following equations <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo>-</mo><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The path of the particle will be
solution:
x=4sinπ2-ωt=4cosωt,

y=4sinωt

Clearly,   x2+y2=4cosωt2+4sinωt2⇒x2+y2=16

Hence, the path covered by particle is a circle.

question: The position, velocity and acceleration of a particle moving with a constant acceleration can be represented by :

subject: Physics
q: Two masses of $3.4$ kg and $2.5$ kg are accelerated from an initial speed of $5$ m/s and $12$ m/s, respectively. The distances traversed by the masses in the $5^\text{th}$ second are $104$ m and $129$ m, respectively. The ratio of their momenta after $10$ s is $\dfrac{x}{8}$. The value of $x$ is ________.
question: Two masses of $3.4$ kg and $2.5$ kg are accelerated from an initial speed of $5$ m/s and $12$ m/s, respectively. The distances traversed by the masses in the $5^\text{th}$ second are $104$ m and $129$ m, respectively. The ratio of their momenta after $10$ s is $\dfrac{x}{8}$. The value of $x$ is ________.
solution:
The distance traversed in the $n^{\text{th}}$ second is given by $S_n = u + \dfrac{a}{2}(2n - 1)$.

For the first mass ($m_1 = 3.4$ kg, $u_1 = 5$ m/s):
$104 = 5 + \dfrac{a_1}{2}(2 \times 5 - 1)$
$99 = \dfrac{9a_1}{2} \Rightarrow a_1 = 22$ m/s$^2$

For the second mass ($m_2 = 2.5$ kg, $u_2 = 12$ m/s):
$129 = 12 + \dfrac{a_2}{2}(2 \times 5 - 1)$
$117 = \dfrac{9a_2}{2} \Rightarrow a_2 = 26$ m/s$^2$

Velocities of the masses after $10$ s:
$v_1 = u_1 + a_1 t = 5 + 22 \times 10 = 225$ m/s
$v_2 = u_2 + a_2 t = 12 + 26 \times 10 = 272$ m/s

Momenta of the masses after $10$ s:
$p_1 = m_1 v_1 = 3.4 \times 225 = 765$ kg m/s
$p_2 = m_2 v_2 = 2.5 \times 272 = 680$ kg m/s

Ratio of their momenta:
$\dfrac{p_1}{p_2} = \dfrac{765}{680} = \dfrac{9}{8}$

Comparing with $\dfrac{x}{8}$, we get $x = 9$.

Answer: $9$

subject: Physics
q: From the top of a tower, a ball is thrown vertically upward which reaches the ground in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. A second ball thrown vertically downward from the same position with the same speed reaches the ground in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. A third ball released, from the rest from the same location, will reach the ground in _____ s.
question: From the top of a tower, a ball is thrown vertically upward which reaches the ground in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. A second ball thrown vertically downward from the same position with the same speed reaches the ground in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. A third ball released, from the rest from the same location, will reach the ground in _____ s.
solution:
When it is thrown upward, -h=v×6-12g62

When it is thrown downward, -h=-v×1.5-12g1.52

Therefore, 5h=536+9 h=45 m

When the ball is dropped, h=12gt2⇒45=12×10×t2⇒t=3 s

Alternate method, t=t1t2=6×1.5=3 s

q: Velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>v</mi></mfenced></math> and acceleration <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>a</mi></mfenced></math> in two systems of units <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> are related as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>n</mi><msup><mi>m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mfrac><msub><mi>a</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mrow><mi>m</mi><mi>n</mi></mrow></mfrac></math> respectively. Here <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> are constants. The relations for distance and time in two systems respectively are
question: Velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>v</mi></mfenced></math> and acceleration <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>a</mi></mfenced></math> in two systems of units <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> are related as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>n</mi><msup><mi>m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mfrac><msub><mi>a</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mrow><mi>m</mi><mi>n</mi></mrow></mfrac></math> respectively. Here <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> are constants. The relations for distance and time in two systems respectively are
solution:
Distance is stated as [L]=v2[a]

 ⇒v22a2=nm2v12a1mn2=n3m3v12a1

Or, we have L2=n3m3L1

Similarly, time T=va

⇒v2a2=nm2v1a1mn=n2mv1a1

So, T2=n2mT1

question: A sportsman runs around a circular track of radius $r$ such that he traverses the path $A B A B$. The distance travelled and displacement, respectively, are

subject: Physics
q: The velocity of a particle is given as $\vec{v} = -x\hat{i} + 2y\hat{j} - z\hat{k}$ m/s. The magnitude of acceleration at point $(1, 2, 4)$ is _______ m/s$^2$.
question: The velocity of a particle is given as $\vec{v} = -x\hat{i} + 2y\hat{j} - z\hat{k}$ m/s. The magnitude of acceleration at point $(1, 2, 4)$ is _______ m/s$^2$.
solution:
The velocity vector is given by $\vec{v} = v_x \hat{i} + v_y \hat{j} + v_z \hat{k} = -x \hat{i} + 2y \hat{j} - z \hat{k}$.

The components of acceleration are:

$a_x = v_x \dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} + v_y \dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial y} + v_z \dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial z} = (-x)(-1) + 0 + 0 = x$

$a_y = v_x \dfrac{\partial v_y}{\partial x} + v_y \dfrac{\partial v_y}{\partial y} + v_z \dfrac{\partial v_y}{\partial z} = 0 + (2y)(2) + 0 = 4y$

$a_z = v_x \dfrac{\partial v_z}{\partial x} + v_y \dfrac{\partial v_z}{\partial y} + v_z \dfrac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} = 0 + 0 + (-z)(-1) = z$

The acceleration vector is $\vec{a} = x \hat{i} + 4y \hat{j} + z \hat{k}$.

At the point $(1, 2, 4)$, substituting $x = 1$, $y = 2$, and $z = 4$:

$\vec{a} = 1 \hat{i} + 4(2) \hat{j} + 4 \hat{k} = \hat{i} + 8 \hat{j} + 4 \hat{k}$

The magnitude of acceleration is:

$|\vec{a}| = \sqrt{1^2 + 8^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{1 + 64 + 16} = \sqrt{81} = 9$ m/s$^2$

Answer: $9$

subject: Physics
q: The velocity of a particle is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>+</mo><mi>g</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>F</mi><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Its position is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mo>;</mo></math> then its displacement after time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is :
question: The velocity of a particle is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>+</mo><mi>g</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>F</mi><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Its position is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mo>;</mo></math> then its displacement after time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is :
solution:
v=v0+gt+Ft2

dsdt=v0+gt+Ft2

∫ds=∫01v0+gt+Ft2dt

s=v0t+gt22+Ft3301

s=v0+g2+F3

subject: Physics
q: A body travels a distance $s$ in $t$ seconds. It starts from rest and ends at rest. In the first part of the journey, it moves with constant acceleration $\mathrm{f}$ and in the second part with constant retardation $\mathrm{r}$. The value of $\mathrm{t}$ is given by
question: A body travels a distance $s$ in $t$ seconds. It starts from rest and ends at rest. In the first part of the journey, it moves with constant acceleration $\mathrm{f}$ and in the second part with constant retardation $\mathrm{r}$. The value of $\mathrm{t}$ is given by
solution:
Portion OA, OB corresponds to motion with acceleration ' $\mathrm{f}$ ' and retardation ' $r$ ' respectively.
Area of $\triangle O A B=S$ and $O B=t$. Let $O L=t_1$,
$\mathrm{LB}=\mathrm{t}_2$ and $\mathrm{AL}=\mathrm{v}, \mathrm{S}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{OB} \cdot \mathrm{AL}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{t} \cdot \mathrm{v} ; \mathrm{v}=\frac{2 \mathrm{~S}}{\mathrm{t}}$
Also, $\mathrm{f}=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{t}_1}, \mathrm{t}_1=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{f}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{~s}}{\mathrm{tf}}$ and $\mathrm{r}=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{t}_2}, \mathrm{t}_2=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{r}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{~s}}{\mathrm{tr}} ; \mathrm{t}=\mathrm{t}_1+\mathrm{t}_2=\frac{2 \mathrm{~s}}{\mathrm{tf}}+\frac{2 \mathrm{~s}}{\mathrm{tr}}$
$t=\left(\frac{1}{f}+\frac{1}{r}\right) \frac{2 s}{t} \Rightarrow t=\sqrt{2 s\left(\frac{1}{f}+\frac{1}{r}\right)}$

subject: Physics
q: A body of mass 2 kg moving with velocity of $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{\mathrm{in}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{j}} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}$ enters into a constant force field of 6 N directed along positive z -axis. If the body remains in the field for a period of $\frac{5}{3}$ seconds, then velocity of the body when it emerges from force field is
question: A body of mass 2 kg moving with velocity of $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{\mathrm{in}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{j}} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}$ enters into a constant force field of 6 N directed along positive z -axis. If the body remains in the field for a period of $\frac{5}{3}$ seconds, then velocity of the body when it emerges from force field is
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{\mathrm{B}}{2} \hat{\mathrm{k}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}, \mathrm{t}=\frac{5}{3} \mathrm{~s} \\ & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{u}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{j}} \\ & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{u}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \mathrm{t}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The velocity-time graph of an object moving along a straight line is shown in figure. What is the distance covered by the object between $t=0$ to $t=4 \mathrm{~s}$ ?
question: The velocity-time graph of an object moving along a straight line is shown in figure. What is the distance covered by the object between $t=0$ to $t=4 \mathrm{~s}$ ?
solution:
Distance travelled = displacement when direction of velocity remains constant
$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \text { Distance } & =\text { Area } \\ & =\frac{1}{2}(2 \mathrm{~s}+4 \mathrm{~s})(10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}) \\ & =30 \mathrm{~m}\end{aligned}$

question: A cyclist starts from the point $P$ of a circular ground of radius $2 \mathrm{~km}$ and travels along its circumference to the point $\mathrm{S}$. The displacement of a cyclist is:
solution: $\therefore$ Displacement $=\mathrm{R} \sqrt{2}=2 \sqrt{2}=\sqrt{8} \mathrm{~km}$

subject: Physics
q: A particle is moving in a straight line such that its velocity is increasing at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> per meter. The acceleration of the particle is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> at a point where its velocity is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
question: A particle is moving in a straight line such that its velocity is increasing at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> per meter. The acceleration of the particle is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> at a point where its velocity is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
solution:
According to the question, velocity is increasing at 5 m s-1. Therefore, dvds=5 s-1. 

Now acceleration a=dvdt=vdvds

⇒a=vdvds=20×5=100 m s-2

subject: Physics
q: The velocity ($v$) versus time ($t$) plot of a particle is shown in the figure, for a time interval of $40\text{ s}$. The total distance travelled by the particle and the average velocity during this period are, respectively _______.
question: The velocity ($v$) versus time ($t$) plot of a particle is shown in the figure, for a time interval of $40\text{ s}$. The total distance travelled by the particle and the average velocity during this period are, respectively _______.
solution:
The total distance travelled by the particle is equal to the sum of the magnitudes of the areas under the velocity-time graph.

Area of the first triangle (from $t = 0$ to $t = 20\text{ s}$) is:
$A_1 = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 20 \times 5 = 50\text{ m}$

Magnitude of the area of the second triangle (from $t = 20$ to $t = 40\text{ s}$) is:
$|A_2| = \left| \dfrac{1}{2} \times 20 \times (-5) \right| = 50\text{ m}$

Total distance $= A_1 + |A_2| = 50 + 50 = 100\text{ m}$

The total displacement is the algebraic sum of the areas under the velocity-time graph.

Total displacement $= A_1 + A_2 = 50 - 50 = 0\text{ m}$

Average velocity is the ratio of total displacement to total time.

Average velocity $= \dfrac{\text{Total displacement}}{\text{Total time}} = \dfrac{0}{40} = 0$

Answer: $100\text{ m}$ and zero

subject: Physics
q: A ball is projected vertically upward with an initial velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>, another ball is projected vertically upward with same velocity. At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo></math>____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>, second ball will meet the first ball <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>.
question: A ball is projected vertically upward with an initial velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>, another ball is projected vertically upward with same velocity. At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo></math>____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>, second ball will meet the first ball <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>.
solution:
Distance covered by ball A is h=502-12×10×22=100-20=80 m

Now, at time t=2 s, velocity of A is vA=50-10×2=30 m s-1

Relative velocity of both balls after 2 s is vrel=50-30=20 m s-1

Here, relative displacement xrel=80 m s-1 and relative accelerationarel=0.

Time taken t=8020=4 s

Time at which they will meet is t=4+2=6 s

subject: Physics
q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfrac></mfenced><mrow><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msub></math> is:
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfrac></mfenced><mrow><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msub></math> is:
solution:
Given that t=x+4

⇒x=t-42=t2-8t+16

Differentiating the above equation with respect to time,

⇒dxdt=2t-8

Putting the value of t=4

⇒dxdt=2×4−8=0

subject: Physics
q: Speeds of two identical cars are $u$ and $4 u$ at the specific instant. The ratio of the respective distances in which the two cars are stopped from that instant is
question: Speeds of two identical cars are $u$ and $4 u$ at the specific instant. The ratio of the respective distances in which the two cars are stopped from that instant is
solution:
Use $u^2=2$ as. a is same for both cases
$$
\mathrm{s}_1=\mathrm{u}^2 / 2 \mathrm{a} ; \mathrm{s}_2=16 \mathrm{u}^2 / 2 \mathrm{a}=16 \mathrm{~s}_1 \Rightarrow \mathrm{s}_1: \mathrm{s}_2=1: 16
$$

question: Two stones are thrown up simultaneously from the edge of a cliff <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>240</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> high with an initial speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> respectively. solution: S1=10t-12gt2. When S1=-240⇒t=8s. So, at t=8 seconds first stone will reach the ground. S2=40t-12gt2. Till t=8 s, S2-S1=30t. But after 8 s S1 is constant =-240. Relative to stone 1 for (t1>8) s displacements of stone 2 , S2+240. And at t=12 s seconds stone will reach the ground. question: The velocity (v) and time (t) graph of a body in a straight line motion is shown in the figure. The point S is at 4.333 seconds. The total distance covered by the body in 6 s is :
solution:
Total distance covered by the body is equal to the modulus of area under graph velocity-time graph

⇒d=12×4×2+4×1+12×4×43+12×2×53

⇒d=4+4+83+53

⇒d=373m

The distance travelled by the particle between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is:
question: The instantaneous velocity of a particle moving in a straight line is given as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>α</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> are constants. The distance travelled by the particle between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is:
solution:
V=αt+βt2

dsdt=αt+βt2

∫S1S2 ds=∫12αt+βt2dt

S2-S1=αt22+βt3312

As the particle is not changing direction So distance = displacement. Distance =α[4-1]2+β[8-1]3

=3α2+7β3

The velocity with which middle point of the train passes the signal post is :
question: An engine of a train, moving with uniform acceleration, passes the signal-post with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math> and the last compartment with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math>. v'2=u2+2ad

v2=v'2+2ad

solving, we get

v'=v2+u22

subject: Physics
q: From the top of a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>64</mn></math> metres high tower, a stone is thrown upwards vertically with the velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>48</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The greatest height (in metres) attained by the stone, assuming the value of the gravitational acceleration <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>32</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>, is:
question: From the top of a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>64</mn></math> metres high tower, a stone is thrown upwards vertically with the velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>48</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The greatest height (in metres) attained by the stone, assuming the value of the gravitational acceleration <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>32</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>, is:
solution:
We know that v2=u2+2gs.

At greatest height v=0

u=48 m/sec

g=-32 m/sec2

S=48×482.32=36 m

Total distance =64 m+36 m=100 m

=100 m

subject: Physics
q: A bus moving along a straight highway with speed of $72 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$ is brought to halt within $4 s$ after applying the brakes. The distance travelled by the bus during this time (Assume the retardation is uniform) is _____m.
question: A bus moving along a straight highway with speed of $72 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$ is brought to halt within $4 s$ after applying the brakes. The distance travelled by the bus during this time (Assume the retardation is uniform) is _____m.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Initial velocity }=u=72 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}=20 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \\ & \mathrm{v}=\mathrm{u}+\text { at } \\ & \Rightarrow 0=20+\mathrm{a} \times 4 \\ & \mathrm{a}=-5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2 \\ & \mathrm{v}^2-\mathrm{u}^2=2 \text { as } \\ & \Rightarrow 0^2-20^2=2(-5) . \mathrm{s} \\ & \mathrm{s}=40 \mathrm{~m}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A balloon is moving up in air vertically above a point A on the ground. When it is a height h1, a girl standing at a distance d (point B) from A (see figure) sees it at an angle 45° with respect to the vertical. When the balloon climbs up a further height h2, it is seen at an angle 60° with respect to the vertical if the girl moves further by a distance 2.464 d (point C). Then the height h2 is (given tan30°=0.5774):
question: A balloon is moving up in air vertically above a point A on the ground. When it is a height h1, a girl standing at a distance d (point B) from A (see figure) sees it at an angle 45° with respect to the vertical. When the balloon climbs up a further height h2, it is seen at an angle 60° with respect to the vertical if the girl moves further by a distance 2.464 d (point C). Then the height h2 is (given tan30°=0.5774):
solution:
t45=h1d

h1=d   .......(i)

h1+h2d+2.464d=tan30°=13=0.5774

h1+h2=(3.464)d×0.5774

h1+h2=2d   .......(ii)

∴  h2=d

subject: Physics
q:
The velocity-time graph of a body moving in a straight line is shown in figure.

 

The ratio of displacement and distance travelled by the body in time 0 to 10 s is
question:
The velocity-time graph of a body moving in a straight line is shown in figure.

 

The ratio of displacement and distance travelled by the body in time 0 to 10 s is
solution:
Area of upper rectangles, which will give us magnitude of the area under the velocity-time graph,

A1=8×2+4×4 =32

Area of lower rectangles, which will give us magnitude of the area under the velocity-time graph,

A2=4×2 +4×2 = 16 

Therefore,

Displacement=A1-A2= 32-16 =16 Distance =A1+A2= 32+16=48 

Hence, the required ratio would be

=1648=13

Calculate the speed of the particle at time <math><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>τ</mi></math> (assume that the particle is at origin at <math><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> )
question: A particle is moving with speed <math><mi>v</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>b</mi><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt></math> along positive <math><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo></math> axis. Calculate the speed of the particle at time <math><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>τ</mi></math> (assume that the particle is at origin at <math><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> )
solution:
v=bx … (1)
⇒dxdt=bx
dxx=bdt
∫0xdxx=∫0tbdt
2x=bt … (2)
From equation (1) and (2)
v=bbt2       
∴at t=τ 
v=b2τ2

solution:
It is given that 

s=2.5t2

Differentiating,

v=dsdt=d2.5t2dt=5t

 At t = 5 s, v= 25 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: The maximum speed of a boat in still water is \(27 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}\). Now this boat is moving downstream in a river flowing at \(9 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}\). A man in the boat throws a ball vertically upwards with speed of \(10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\). Range of the ball as observed by an observer at rest on the river bank, is _______ cm. (Take \(\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\))
question: The maximum speed of a boat in still water is \(27 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}\). Now this boat is moving downstream in a river flowing at \(9 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}\). A man in the boat throws a ball vertically upwards with speed of \(10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\). Range of the ball as observed by an observer at rest on the river bank, is _______ cm. (Take \(\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\))
solution:
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{\mathrm{b}}=9+27=36 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr}$
$\begin{aligned} & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{\mathrm{b}}=36 \times \frac{1000}{36000}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec} \\ & \text { Time of flight }=\frac{2 \times 10}{10}=2 \mathrm{sec} \\ & \text { Range }=10 \times 2=20 \mathrm{~m}=2000 \mathrm{~cm}\end{aligned}$

q: The position vector of a particle changes with time according to the relation <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>15</mn><msup><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo>-</mo><mn>20</mn><msup><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>.</mo></math> What is the magnitude of the acceleration at <math><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>?</mo></math>
question: The position vector of a particle changes with time according to the relation <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>15</mn><msup><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo>-</mo><mn>20</mn><msup><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>.</mo></math> What is the magnitude of the acceleration at <math><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>?</mo></math>
solution:
r→=15t2i^+4-20t2j^
v→=30 ti^-40t j^
a→=30 i^-40j^
At t=1 ,
a→=30 i^-40j^
a→=50

subject: Physics
q: A particle is moving in a straight line. The variation of position <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> as a function of time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> is given as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>20</mn><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>15</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The velocity of the body when its acceleration becomes zero is:
question: A particle is moving in a straight line. The variation of position <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> as a function of time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> is given as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>20</mn><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>15</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The velocity of the body when its acceleration becomes zero is:
solution:
Given the instantaneous position is

x=t3-6t2+20t+15   ...1

The velocity of the particle is given by

v=dxdt=3t2-12t+20   ...2

And, the acceleration of the particle is given by

a=dvdt=6t-12   ...3

When a=0, it implies

6t-12=0⇒t=2 s

At t=2 s, the velocity can be calculated as follows:

v=3(2)2-12(2)+20=8 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: The graph of an object's motion (along the $x-$ axis) is shown in the figure. The instantaneous velocity of the object at points $A$ and $B$ are $v_A$ and $v_B$ respectively. Then
question: The graph of an object's motion (along the $x-$ axis) is shown in the figure. The instantaneous velocity of the object at points $A$ and $B$ are $v_A$ and $v_B$ respectively. Then
solution: Instantaneous velocity $v=\frac{\Delta x}{\Delta t}$ From graph, $v_A=\frac{\Delta x_A}{\Delta t_A}=\frac{4 m}{8 s}=0.5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ and $v_B=\frac{\Delta x_B}{\Delta t_B}=\frac{8 m}{16 s}=0.5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ i.e., $v_A=v_B=0.5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
